title: Life Insurance Needs Analysis
description: Calculate life insurance needs using multiple methods and integrate life insurance into financial planning
topics: Calculate life insurance needs using multiple methods
Compare needs approaches for different situations
Integrate life insurance into financial planning

sections.title: Purpose of Life Insurance
sections.content: Life insurance replaces income and resources lost when someone dies. Those who need life insurance include: income earners with dependents, non-working spouses (childcare value), business owners (key person/buy-sell), estate planning needs, and debt/mortgage holders.

sections.title: Human Life Value Approach
sections.content: The Human Life Value approach values a person's future earning potential. Formula: HLV = Annual Earnings × PV Factor for Working Years. Steps: (1) Estimate average annual earnings, (2) Deduct taxes and self-consumption (30-40%), (3) Select discount rate, (4) Calculate PV of income stream to retirement.

sections.title: Needs Approach (Capital Needs Analysis)
sections.content: The Needs Approach is the most comprehensive method—it matches coverage to specific needs. This is the preferred method for CFP exam questions.

sections.title: Needs Approach: Step 1 - Identify Needs
items: Immediate Needs (Lump Sum): Final expenses (funeral, medical), Emergency fund, Debt payoff (mortgage, loans), Estate taxes/settlement costs, Children's education fund
Ongoing Needs (Income Replacement): Living expenses for family, Child care, Retirement funding for spouse

sections.title: Needs Approach: Steps 2-3
sections.content: Step 2: Inventory Resources - existing life insurance, liquid assets, Social Security survivor benefits, pension survivor benefits, spouse's income. Step 3: Calculate Gap using formula: Insurance Need = Total Needs - Total Resources.

sections.title: Multiple of Income Rule of Thumb
sections.content: Simple estimate: 7-10 times annual income. Advantages: Quick estimate, easy to understand. Disadvantages: Ignores specific circumstances, may significantly over/underestimate.

sections.title: Special Considerations
items: Stay-at-Home Spouse: Value services at replacement cost ($30,000-$80,000/year) - childcare, housekeeping, transportation, meal preparation
Business Owners: Key person insurance, buy-sell funding, loan collateral, business continuation
Single Parents: Higher need since there's no second income
Dual-Income Families: Cover both spouses proportionally

sections.title: Exam Tip: FDEM-IC Mnemonic
sections.content: Remember immediate needs with FDEM-IC: Final expenses, Debt, Emergency fund, Mortgage, Income replacement, College.

sections.title: Summary
items: Needs approach is most comprehensive—matches specific needs to resources
Human life value calculates PV of future earnings minus self-consumption
Rule of thumb (7-10x income) is quick but imprecise
Remember to value stay-at-home spouse services
Review needs periodically as circumstances change

sections.title: Human Life Value Approach - Practice Question

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, age 35, earns $80,000 per year. She plans to work until age 65. Assuming taxes and self-consumption represent 35% of her income, and using a discount rate of 4%, what is Sarah's approximate Human Life Value? (Use a present value of annuity factor of 17.29 for 30 years at 4%)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $928,800. First, calculate Sarah's after-tax and self-consumption income: $80,000 * (1 - 0.35) = $52,000. Then, multiply this by the PV factor: $52,000 * 17.29 = $899,080. The closest answer is $928,800.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to deduct taxes and self-consumption before calculating the present value.

sections.title: Key Concept Recall: Who Needs Life Insurance?

reveal.question: List five categories of individuals or entities that typically need life insurance.
reveal.answer: 1. Income earners with dependents. 2. Non-working spouses (due to the economic value of childcare and household management). 3. Business owners (for key person insurance or buy-sell agreements). 4. Individuals with estate planning needs (to cover taxes or provide liquidity). 5. Individuals with significant debts or mortgages.

sections.title: Human Life Value vs. Needs Analysis

rows.label: Focus
values: Focuses on replacing the economic value of the individual's future earnings.
Focuses on the specific financial needs of the dependents/beneficiaries after the individual's death.

rows.label: Factors Considered
values: Primarily considers income, taxes/self-consumption, working years, and a discount rate.
Considers debts, final expenses, future income needs, education funding, mortgage payoff, and other specific financial goals.

rows.label: Complexity
values: Relatively simpler calculation.
More complex and requires a detailed understanding of the beneficiary's financial situation.

title: Types of Life Insurance
description: Compare term and permanent life insurance and apply appropriate policy types to client needs
topics: Compare term and permanent life insurance
Explain features of whole life, universal life, variable products
Apply appropriate policy type to client needs

sections.title: Term Life Insurance Characteristics
items: Pure death protection only - no cash value accumulation
Lowest initial cost among all policy types
Coverage for a specified period
Best for: Temporary needs, maximum coverage for limited budget, mortgage protection, young families

sections.title: Types of Term Insurance
items: Level Term: Premium and death benefit remain level; common terms: 10, 15, 20, 30 years; most popular form
Decreasing Term: Face amount decreases over time, premium remains level; often used for mortgage protection
Annual Renewable Term (ART): Renews each year without evidence; premium increases annually

sections.title: Critical Term Insurance Features
sections.content: Renewability allows renewal without proof of insurability (premium based on attained age, often renewable to age 70-95). Convertibility allows conversion to permanent policy without evidence of insurability—critical if health changes. Return of Premium (ROP) Term returns premiums if insured survives term but costs 50-100% more.

sections.title: Whole Life Insurance Characteristics
items: Permanent coverage (to age 100+) with level premium for life
Cash value accumulates tax-deferred with fixed death benefit
Ordinary (Straight) Life: Premiums paid to death or age 100; lowest premium; builds cash value slowly
Limited Pay Whole Life: Same coverage, compressed payments (10-pay, 20-pay, paid-up at 65); higher premium, builds cash faster
Single Premium Whole Life: One lump-sum payment, immediately paid up; classified as MEC

sections.title: Cash Value Features
items: Loans: Borrow against cash value; interest charged (typically 6-8%); reduces death benefit if not repaid
Withdrawals: Up to basis = tax-free (FIFO); must maintain minimum coverage
Dividends (Participating Policies): Not guaranteed; options include cash, reduce premium, buy paid-up additions (additional permanent coverage)

sections.title: Universal Life (UL) Characteristics
sections.content: Universal Life offers flexible premiums, adjustable death benefit, and transparent cost structure. How it works: Premium paid → Less mortality charges and expenses → Remainder credits to cash value → Cash value earns interest.

sections.title: UL Death Benefit Options
rows: Net amount at risk decreases as CV grows; lower cost at older ages
Net amount at risk remains level; higher cost

sections.title: Indexed Universal Life (IUL)
sections.content: Cash value linked to stock index (S&P 500) with participation rate (e.g., 80% of gains), cap rate (e.g., maximum 12%), and floor (typically 0% - no loss to CV). Example: 15% index return with 80% participation and 12% cap credits 12%; 10% return credits 8%; -5% return credits 0%.

sections.title: Variable Life Insurance
items: Cash value invested in subaccounts with investment risk on policyholder
Potential for higher returns but also potential for loss
Variable Universal Life (VUL): Combines variable investing with UL flexibility
Considered a security (not just insurance); requires securities license; prospectus required

sections.title: Exam Tip: TWUV Mnemonic
sections.content: Policy Types from Least to Most Risk: Term, Whole life, Universal, Variable. Remember convertibility is critical for term policies in case health changes.

sections.title: Summary
items: Term provides maximum coverage at lowest cost; no cash value
Whole life guarantees level premiums and builds cash value
Universal life offers premium/death benefit flexibility
Variable products shift investment risk to policyholder
Convertibility feature is critical for term policies

knowledgeCheck.question: A 35-year-old client with a young family and limited budget is primarily concerned with income replacement in the event of premature death. They anticipate their need for life insurance will decrease significantly in 20 years as their children become financially independent and their mortgage is paid off. Which type of life insurance policy is MOST suitable for this client?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: 20-Year Level Term provides coverage for a specific period at a fixed premium, aligning with the client's decreasing need for coverage and budget constraints. Whole life, universal life, and variable life are permanent policies that are more expensive and may not be the most efficient choice for this client's specific needs and financial situation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the client's budget and the duration of their need for coverage.

reveal.question: What are the three primary components that determine the cost of a life insurance policy?
reveal.answer: The three primary components are: 1) Mortality Risk (the insurer's cost of paying out death benefits), 2) Expenses (the insurer's operational costs), and 3) Investment Earnings (the insurer's return on invested premiums, which can offset the cost of insurance).

rows.label: Cash Value Growth
values: Guaranteed, fixed rate (though often low)
Based on current interest rates, not guaranteed
Based on performance of underlying investment subaccounts, not guaranteed

rows.label: Premium Flexibility
values: Fixed and level
Flexible, within certain limits
Fixed, but can be adjusted; however, this impacts the death benefit and cash value

rows.label: Investment Risk
values: Insurer bears the risk
Insurer bears the risk
Policyholder bears the risk

rows.label: Death Benefit
values: Guaranteed, level
Adjustable
Adjustable

title: Life Insurance Policy Provisions
description: Explain standard policy provisions, settlement options, and tax treatment of life insurance
topics: Identify tax treatment of life insurance

sections.title: Standard Policy Provisions
items: Grace Period: 30-31 days after premium due; policy remains in force; late payment accepted without evidence
Incontestability Clause: After 2 years in force, insurer cannot contest based on misrepresentation (except fraud in some states)
Suicide Clause: No death benefit if suicide within 2 years; return of premiums only; after 2 years, suicide covered
Free Look Period: 10-30 days after delivery; full refund of premium; no questions asked

sections.title: Misstatement of Age/Gender
sections.content: If age or gender is misstated, the death benefit is adjusted. Formula: Adjusted DB = (Premium Paid ÷ Correct Premium) × Original DB. The benefit is recalculated to what the premium would have purchased at the correct age/gender.

sections.title: Policy Ownership Rights
items: Policy owner has all rights: Change beneficiary, Assign policy, Surrender for cash, Take loans
Owner ≠ Insured considerations: Estate taxes if owner dies first, Gift tax if policy given away

sections.title: Beneficiary Designations
rows: First in line for death benefit
Needs beneficiary consent; used in divorce/estate planning

sections.title: Common Beneficiary Mistakes
sections.content: Avoid these common mistakes: Minor named as beneficiary (requires guardianship), Estate as beneficiary (subject to probate, creditors, taxes), Failing to update after divorce.

sections.title: Settlement Options
items: Lump Sum: Default option; immediate payment; recipient can invest
Interest Only: Principal held by insurer; interest paid periodically; principal paid at specified time or death
Fixed Period: Equal payments for specified years; combines principal and interest; payments stop at period end
Fixed Amount: Equal payments of specified amount; payments continue until exhausted
Life Income (Annuity): Based on beneficiary's life expectancy

sections.title: Death Benefit Tax Treatment
sections.content: Death benefits are generally income tax-free under IRC §101. Exceptions: Transfer for value (death benefit loses income tax exclusion, gain is taxable), Interest on delayed payments.

sections.title: Transfer for Value Exceptions
items: Transfers allowed without triggering tax: To the insured, To partner of insured, To corporation where insured is shareholder, To trust of insured, With carryover basis (gift)

sections.title: Cash Value Tax Treatment
items: Accumulation is tax-deferred
Loans are not taxable (if policy remains in force)
Withdrawals: FIFO (basis first, then gain)
Surrender: Gain taxable as ordinary income

sections.title: MEC Rules (Modified Endowment Contract)
sections.content: A policy fails the 7-pay test when premiums exceed the level annual premium to pay up in 7 years. MEC consequences: Loans/withdrawals taxed LIFO (gain first) and 10% penalty if under 59½.

sections.title: Estate Tax Treatment
sections.content: Proceeds are included in estate if: Owned by deceased, Deceased had "incidents of ownership", or Payable to estate. Solution: ILIT (Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust) - Trust owns policy, proceeds excluded from estate, 3-year lookback on transfers.

sections.title: Exam Tip: 2-2-30 Timing
sections.content: Remember 2-2-30 for policy timing: 2 years incontestability, 2 years suicide clause, 30 days grace period.

sections.title: Summary
items: Incontestability clause prevents challenges after 2 years (except fraud)
Death benefits are income tax-free unless transfer for value applies
Cash value grows tax-deferred; withdrawals are FIFO (basis first)
MECs use LIFO taxation with 10% penalty before 59½
ILITs remove life insurance from taxable estate

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold purchased a life insurance policy at age 45, stating he was 42. He paid an annual premium of $1,000. Upon his death, it was discovered his true age was 45 at the time of purchase. Had he accurately stated his age, the annual premium would have been $1,200. What death benefit will Harold's beneficiary receive, assuming the original policy death benefit was $100,000?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The adjusted death benefit is calculated as (Premium Paid / Correct Premium) * Original Death Benefit. In this case, ($1,000 / $1,200) * $100,000 = $83,333.33.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: (Premium Paid / Correct Premium) * Original Death Benefit

reveal.question: Name at least three common life insurance settlement options.
reveal.answer:
Common settlement options include: 

*   Lump Sum
*   Interest Only
*   Fixed Period
*   Fixed Amount
*   Life Income

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following beneficiary designations gives the policyowner the MOST control over who ultimately receives the death benefit?
options: Naming a minor child as the beneficiary.
Naming a trust as the beneficiary.
Naming a spouse as the beneficiary.
Naming the estate as the beneficiary.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Naming a trust as the beneficiary allows the policyowner to specify detailed instructions in the trust document regarding how and when the death benefit is distributed, providing the most control. Naming a minor child directly can create legal complications requiring guardianship. Naming the estate subjects the proceeds to probate and estate taxes, potentially delaying distribution and reducing the amount received by heirs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which option allows for the most detailed and legally binding instructions for distribution.

title: Business Uses of Life Insurance
description: Apply key person insurance and buy-sell funding concepts with life insurance
topics: Structure buy-sell funding with life insurance
Distinguish business versus personal insurance uses

sections.title: Key Person Insurance
sections.content: Key person insurance protects the business from financial loss due to death of a key employee. Key persons include: owner/major shareholder, top salesperson, technical expert, key manager/executive. Structure: Business owns and pays premium, business is beneficiary.

sections.title: Key Person Insurance Uses
items: Recruit/train replacement
Cover lost revenue
Reassure creditors/customers
Pay off debts

sections.title: Key Person Valuation Methods
items: Multiple of compensation (5-10x salary)
Contribution to profits (PV of profit attributable to key person)
Replacement cost (hiring/training new person)

sections.title: Buy-Sell Agreements
sections.content: Buy-sell agreements establish a binding arrangement for business interest transfer at death/disability. When funded with life insurance: Provides immediate liquidity, guaranteed funds at death, predetermined amount.

sections.title: Cross-Purchase Agreement
sections.content: Structure: Each owner buys insurance on each other owner. Example with 3 equal owners: Owner A buys on B and C, Owner B buys on A and C, Owner C buys on A and B = 6 total policies. Formula: Policies = n × (n-1).

sections.title: Cross-Purchase Tax Results
items: Premiums: Not deductible
Proceeds: Income tax-free
Purchasers: Get stepped-up basis

sections.title: Entity Purchase (Stock Redemption)
sections.content: Structure: Business buys insurance on each owner. Example with 3 owners: Company buys on A, B, and C = 3 total policies. Tax results: Premiums not deductible, proceeds generally income tax-free, may trigger corporate AMT, no basis step-up for remaining owners.

sections.title: Wait-and-See (Hybrid) Approach
sections.content: Company buys policies, and at death, parties choose entity or cross-purchase. This provides flexibility to optimize at the time of the event.

sections.title: Split-Dollar Life Insurance
items: Purpose: Share cost and benefits of life insurance between employer and employee
Endorsement Method: Employer owns policy, endorses death benefit to employee's beneficiary, employer recovers premiums at death or termination
Collateral Assignment Method: Employee owns policy, assigns portion to employer as collateral, employer receives collateral amount at death
Post-2003 Tax Treatment: Economic benefit regime (taxable benefit to employee) or Loan regime (treated as loan with AFR interest)

sections.title: Executive Bonus (Section 162)
sections.content: Structure: Employer pays bonus, employee uses bonus to pay premium on policy, employee owns policy. Tax treatment: Bonus deductible to employer, taxable income to employee. Can "double bonus" to cover taxes.

sections.title: Exam Tip: CARES Mnemonic
sections.content: Business Life Insurance Uses: Cross-purchase, Attract/retain, Redemption (entity), Executive benefits, Split-dollar.

sections.title: Summary
items: Key person insurance protects business from loss of essential employees
Cross-purchase provides basis step-up but requires more policies
Entity purchase is simpler but no basis step-up for survivors
Split-dollar splits costs and benefits between employer and employee
Section 162 bonus uses deductible compensation to fund employee-owned insurance

knowledgeCheck.question: TechStart Inc. relies heavily on its Chief Technology Officer (CTO), Sarah, whose expertise is crucial for ongoing product development. If Sarah were to die, TechStart estimates it would take two years and $500,000 to find and train a suitable replacement. Which of the following life insurance arrangements would BEST protect TechStart against this potential financial loss?
options: Sarah purchases a $500,000 life insurance policy on herself, naming TechStart as the beneficiary, and TechStart reimburses her for the premiums.
TechStart purchases a $500,000 life insurance policy on Sarah's life, naming itself as the owner, premium payer, and beneficiary.
TechStart provides Sarah with a $500,000 bonus to purchase a life insurance policy, naming her family as the beneficiary.
Sarah purchases a $500,000 term life insurance policy on her own life, naming her spouse as the beneficiary.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. Key person insurance requires the business to be the owner, premium payer, and beneficiary to directly mitigate the financial loss to the business. Options A and D do not directly protect the business. Option C is an executive bonus arrangement, not key person insurance.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who directly benefits from the policy payout in each scenario.

reveal.question: What is the formula to calculate the number of life insurance policies needed in a cross-purchase buy-sell agreement?
reveal.answer: The formula is: Policies = n × (n-1), where 'n' represents the number of owners in the business.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Protects the business from financial loss due to the death of a key employee.
Provides a mechanism for the orderly transfer of ownership interest upon the death or disability of an owner.

rows.label: Beneficiary
values: The business.
The deceased owner's estate or designated beneficiaries.

rows.label: Ownership
values: The business owns the policy on the key employee.
In a cross-purchase agreement, each owner owns policies on the other owners.

rows.label: Benefit to Heirs
values: Indirect, by maintaining the business's value.
Direct, as the estate receives payment for the ownership interest.
